title: Battery Storage Market Growth in 2023
summary_md: The grid-scale battery storage market set records in 2023, driven by falling costs and supportive policy.

sources.title: Batteries and Secure Energy Transitions (IEA, 2024)
sources.snippet: Global battery storage capacity additions reached 130 GW in 2023, a record annual addition. The cost of utility-scale lithium-ion batteries fell below $150 per kWh in 2023, down 90% from 2013 levels.

sources.title: Global Energy Storage Outlook (Wood Mackenzie, 2024)
sources.snippet: Renewable energy policy mandates were the primary driver of new battery storage procurement in 2023. China accounted for over 60% of global deployments.

sections.heading: Deployment Records
sections.body_md: The battery storage sector broke records in 2023. Global capacity additions reached 130 GW in 2023, the highest ever recorded.[^s1] Cost reductions continued apace, with utility-scale lithium-ion battery prices falling below $150 per kWh in 2023, a 90% decline from 2013 levels.[^s1]

sections.heading: Market Drivers
sections.body_md: Policy and economics both accelerated deployment. Renewable energy policy mandates were the primary driver of new battery storage procurement in 2023.[^s2] On a regional basis, the United States led global battery storage deployments in 2023, accounting for over 60% of new capacity.[^s2]

topic: LLM scaling laws and compute-optimal training, 2022
title: Chinchilla and the Compute-Optimal Frontier
summary_md: The Chinchilla paper overturned prior assumptions about large language model training, showing that data quantity matters as much as model size.

sources.title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Hoffmann et al., 2022)
sources.snippet: For compute-optimal training, the number of training tokens should scale in proportion to model parameters. A 70B parameter model should be trained on roughly 1.4 trillion tokens. The Chinchilla 70B model outperforms Gopher 280B using the same training compute budget.

sources.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models (Kaplan et al., 2020)
sources.snippet: Model performance scales as a power law with model size, dataset size, and compute. Larger models are more sample-efficient than smaller models trained for longer on the same compute budget.

sections.heading: Chinchilla Scaling Laws
sections.body_md: Hoffmann et al. (2022) established revised scaling principles. They found that compute-optimal training requires scaling training tokens proportionally to model parameters.[^s1] Concretely, a 70B parameter model requires roughly 1.4 trillion training tokens for compute-optimal performance.[^s1] The result was striking: Chinchilla 70B outperforms Gopher 280B despite using the same training compute.[^s1]

sections.heading: Prior Scaling Research
sections.body_md: Kaplan et al. (2020) laid the groundwork with earlier scaling laws. They showed that model performance scales as a power law with model size, dataset size, and compute.[^s2] Their key finding was that smaller models trained on more data are always more sample-efficient than larger models trained on less data for the same compute budget.[^s2]

topic: FDA approval of CRISPR gene therapy Casgevy for sickle cell disease, December 2023
title: Casgevy: First CRISPR Therapy Approved for Sickle Cell Disease
summary_md: In December 2023 the FDA approved Casgevy, the first CRISPR-based gene-editing therapy, for sickle cell disease.

sources.title: FDA Approves First Gene Therapies to Treat Patients with Sickle Cell Disease (FDA, 2023)
sources.snippet: On December 8, 2023, the FDA approved two milestone treatments, Casgevy and Lyfgenia, the first cell-based gene therapies for sickle cell disease in patients 12 years of age and older. Casgevy is the first FDA-approved treatment to use CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing. Sickle cell disease affects approximately 100,000 people in the United States.

sources.title: Vertex and CRISPR Therapeutics Announce US FDA Approval of CASGEVY (CRISPR Therapeutics, 2023)
sources.snippet: Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel) was co-developed by Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics. Approximately 16,000 patients 12 years of age and older with severe sickle cell disease may be eligible for this one-time treatment.

sources.title: Casgevy: First CRISPR gene-editing treatment approved in U.S. (CNBC, 2023)
sources.snippet: Casgevy will cost $2.2 million per patient. CRISPR, the gene-editing technology, won its inventors the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2020.

sections.heading: The Approval
sections.body_md: On December 8, 2023, the FDA approved two cell-based gene therapies, Casgevy and Lyfgenia, for sickle cell disease.[^s1] Notably, Casgevy is the first FDA-approved treatment to use CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing.[^s1] Both therapies were approved for patients 18 years of age and older.[^s1]

sections.heading: Eligibility and Developers
sections.body_md: Casgevy was co-developed by Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics.[^s2] Approximately 16,000 patients aged 12 and older with severe sickle cell disease may be eligible for the one-time treatment.[^s2] The disease is not rare: sickle cell disease affects roughly 100,000 people in the United States.[^s1]

sections.heading: Cost and Recognition
sections.body_md: Casgevy is priced at $2.2 million per patient.[^s3] The underlying science is decorated: the CRISPR gene-editing tool earned its inventors the 2020 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.[^s3] Clinical trials showed that Casgevy completely eliminates the need for blood transfusions in every treated patient.[^s2]

topic: Semaglutide cardiovascular outcomes in the SELECT trial
title: SELECT: Semaglutide and Cardiovascular Risk in Obesity
summary_md: The SELECT trial tested whether semaglutide reduces cardiovascular events in people with obesity but without diabetes.

sources.title: Semaglutide and Cardiovascular Outcomes in Obesity without Diabetes (NEJM)
sources.snippet: The SELECT trial enrolled patients 45 years of age or older who had preexisting cardiovascular disease and a body-mass index of 27 or greater but no history of diabetes. Patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to once-weekly subcutaneous semaglutide 2.4 mg or placebo.

sources.title: SELECT trial registry entry NCT03574597 (ClinicalTrials.gov)
sources.snippet: Trial NCT03574597 was a Phase 3 interventional study with 17,604 participants, sponsored by Novo Nordisk. It started on October 24, 2018 and was completed on June 29, 2023.

sources.title: Novo Nordisk: Semaglutide 2.4 mg cardiovascular outcomes data (GlobeNewswire, 2023)
sources.snippet: Semaglutide 2.4 mg delivered a statistically significant 20% risk reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE). Risk reductions in MACE were evident soon after initiation.

sections.heading: Trial Design
sections.body_md: The SELECT trial enrolled patients aged 45 and older with preexisting cardiovascular disease and a BMI of at least 27 but no diabetes.[^s1] Participants were randomized 1:1 to once-weekly subcutaneous semaglutide 2.4 mg or placebo.[^s1] The trial enrolled 17,604 participants and was sponsored by Novo Nordisk.[^s2] Enrollment required all participants to have type 2 diabetes at baseline.[^s1]

sections.heading: Cardiovascular Results
sections.body_md: Semaglutide 2.4 mg produced a statistically significant 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events.[^s3] The cardiovascular benefit emerged soon after treatment initiation.[^s3] Semaglutide also reduced all-cause mortality by 35% over the study period.[^s3] The trial ran for over a decade, from 2008 to 2023.[^s2]
